paper_title: Experimental Design and Microelectrode Measurements in Marine Organisms

methods.description: A laboratory experiment designed to study the effects of temperature and pCO2 on marine organisms, using a crossed-factor design with multiple treatments and replicates.

steps.description: Natural oligotrophic seawater collected from Svalbard, Norway, was used.
Seawater in all experimental tanks was equilibrated with mixed gases formulated at three pCO2 levels.
steps.searchable_summary: equilibrate seawater with mixed gases at specified pCO2 levels

steps.description: Seawater was continuously pumped from the lower large reservoir to the four replicate tanks contained within the upper large reservoir of each treatment group.
steps.searchable_summary: pump seawater between reservoirs and replicate tanks

steps.description: Salinity, pH, and temperature were measured three times per week in all of the replicate tanks using a combination probe kit.
steps.searchable_summary: measure salinity, pH, and temperature in tanks

steps.description: Concentration of carbonate ions, bicarbonate ions, aqueous CO2, aragonite (ΩA), and calcite saturation states (ΩC) were calculated from measured TA, DIC, salinity, and temperature with CO2SYS software.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate carbonate chemistry parameters using CO2SYS

methods.description: Procedure for measuring pH gradients in the diffusive boundary layer (DBL) of foraminifera using liquid ion exchange (LIX) pH microelectrodes.

steps.description: LIX pH microelectrodes were fabricated and calibrated in pH buffer solutions at treatment temperature.
steps.searchable_summary: fabricate and calibrate LIX pH microelectrodes

steps.description: Microelectrode tips were positioned on the LBF test surface using a motorized micromanipulator.
steps.searchable_summary: position microelectrode tips on test surface

steps.description: The gradient in pH away from the test surface was measured in three specimens per treatment.
steps.searchable_summary: measure pH gradient from test surface

steps.description: The difference in [H+] between the DBL and the seawater was calculated as an indicator of the specimen's mitigation of acidified conditions.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate [H+] difference between DBL and seawater
